title: GetRootlessRuntimeDir does not check for readonly FS
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

As noted in https://github.com/bazelbuild/rules_docker/issues/1663, when podman tries to figure out where to store its runtime files it ends up choosing /run/user/ID. But when running under bazel this directory may be readonly.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Configure your system such that `which docker` resolves to podman. 

2. Attempt to build bazel rules that execute docker. One example BUILD file might be:

```
load("@io_bazel_rules_docker//docker/package_managers:apt_key.bzl", "add_apt_key")
add_apt_key(
    name = "some_apt_key",
    image = "@debian_buster_slim//image",
    keys = ["DEADBEEF.asc"],
)
```
via a command like `bazel build --sandbox_debug //some_apt_key`

3. Observe a failure by podman attempting to set the sticky bit in a readonly filesystem.

**Describe the results you received:**

Podman fails trying to use a readonly filesystem.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Podman succeeds by choosing a more appropriate filesystem. I suspect this can be resolved by modifying https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/54dce001cce131bbbdcf16512a4722e080561fc7/pkg/util/utils_supported.go#L28 to be more robust to readonly file systems and containerization. Perhaps attempting to create a folder as is done for os.Tempfile() (which should succeed in this case of running under `bazel`).

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.0.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
Not sure how to extract this from the sandbox, if desired I will work on finding it.
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
3.0.1+dfsg1-2
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Why would XDG_RUNTIME_DIR point to a Read/Only file system.  Isn't it your responsibility to launch the container with XDG_RUNTIME_DIR pointing at a writable directory.  If XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is not set, then Podman will attempt to write to files in /tmp, I believe.

XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is unset, sorry the link may be misleading. I mean the fallback code that checks /run/user/ID where it only checks permissions which in this case are succeeding even though the filesystem is read only.
Checking bazel it appears to just be a simple RO bind mount, you can see the effect (writable permissions, but ro filesystem) by running `mount --bind -o ro /some/writable/path /tmp/ro_vers`

While we can look into rewriting the code for choosing a temporary directory to be more robust, this strikes me as more of a problem with Bazel. Why is it remounting important system directories as read-only? We greatly prefer `/run/user` because it's actually guaranteed to be a tmpfs, unlike `/tmp`.

It looks like bazel will take all existing mounts and expose them in the sandbox as read only, /run/user/UID is only added because it was identified as an existing host mount. I could work around this by explicitly adding this directory as writable in the sandbox.

`bazel build --sandbox_writable_path=/run/user/${UID} ...` (I also had to add the container storage to sandbox writable as well)

There does seem to be some code to ensure `/tmp` prefixed paths are writable, so it may be correct to adjust bazel to also ensure `/var/user/${UID}` mounts are also writable, ill bring it up over there.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This looks like it is no longer an issue with Podman, So closing. Reopen if I am mistaken.
